what: Estimation of the steady-state Bayesian Vector Autoregression model using Hamiltonian Monte Carlo
title: Estimation of the steady-state Bayesian Vector Autoregression model using Hamiltonian Monte Carlo
text: package, user can estimate steady-state BVAR(p) model Mattias Villani (Villani, 2009). estimation, user can produce forecasts (unconditional conditional) impulse response functions (orthogonalized generalized). goal use modern Bayesian tools (Stan) : ) estimate model specified original paper, ii) extend model different ways. Back day, extensions model seemed limited Mattias Villani time derive. See example, Clark (2011): “methodological sense, paper extends estimator Villani (2009) include stochastic volatility.” Later , writes: “(Special thanks due Mattias Villani providing formulas posterior means variances Π\Piand Ψ\Psi, generalize constant-variance formulas Villani 2009.)” recent example model mercy Professor Villani, can take look Technical Guide BEAR (Bayesian Estimation, Analysis Regression) toolbox ECB. page 122, Dieppe, Legrand, van Roye (2018) write: “Villani (2009) provides derivation case normal-diffuse prior distribution, incoming analysis restricted case.” Times different now, help Stan, can basically whatever can imagine. showcase , last section build steady-state BVAR model Clark (2011) [extension original model (Villani, 2009)], letting log volatilities follow correlated AR(1) processes instead uncorrelated driftless random walks. done without asking Professor Villani derivations. simply wrote model equations, put priors parameters, Stan rest!

title: Estimation of the steady-state Bayesian Vector Autoregression model using Hamiltonian Monte Carlo
text: can install development version SteadyStateBVAR GitHub :
code: # install.packages("pak") pak::pak("markjwbecker/SteadyStateBVAR")

title: Estimation of the steady-state Bayesian Vector Autoregression model using Hamiltonian Monte Carlo
text: steady-state BVAR(pp) model (Villani, 2009) yt=Ψdt+Π1(yt−1−Ψdt−1)+…+Πp(yt−p−Ψdt−p)+uty_t = \Psi d_t + \Pi_1(y_{t-1}-\Psi d_{t-1})+\dots+\Pi_p(y_{t-p}-\Psi d_{t-p})+u_t yty_t kk-dimensional vector endogenous variables (time series) time tt, dtd_t qq-dimensional vector deterministic (exogenous) variables time tt, (reduced-form) innovations ut∼Nk(0,Σu)u_t \sim N_k(0,\Sigma_u) independence time periods. Πℓ\Pi_\ell ℓ=1,…,p\ell=1,\dots,p (k×k)(k \times k) matrix, Ψ\Psi (k×q)(k \times q) matrix. Now E(yt)=μt=Ψdt\mathrm{E}(y_t)=\mu_t=\Psi d_t unconditional mean, steady state, process. Since long-horizon forecasts stationary VARs converge unconditional mean (steady state), naturally important forecasting perspective obtain precise inference Ψ\Psi. Note current version package allows dtd_t contain either constant, constant dummy variable, constant time trend. can stack (transposed) Πi\Pi_i matrices (kp×k)(kp \times k) matrix β\beta β=[Π1′⋮Πp′]\beta= \begin{bmatrix} \Pi'_1 \\ \vdots  \\ \Pi'_p \end{bmatrix} can rewrite model nonlinear regression (Karlsson, 2013) yt′=dt′Ψ′+[wt′−qt′(Ip⊗Ψ′)]β+ut′y_t' =d_t'\Psi' + \left[w_t'-q_t'(I_p \otimes \Psi') \right]\beta +u_t' wt′=(yt−1′,…,yt−p′)w_t'=(y_{t-1}',\dots,y_{t-p}') kpkp-dimensional vector lagged endogenous variables qt′=(dt−1′,…,dt−p′)q_t'=(d_{t-1}',\dots,d_{t-p}') qpqp-dimensional vector lagged deterministic (exogenous) variables, IpI_p (p×p)(p \times p) identity matrix ⊗\otimes denotes Kronecker product. likelihood written Stan code. goal estimate β,Ψ\beta, \Psi, Σu\Sigma_u, priors needed. First, prior independence β,Ψ\beta, \Psi Σu\Sigma_u assumed. Starting β\beta, use Minnesota prior vec(β)∼Nkpk[θβ,Ωβ]\mathrm{vec}(\beta) \sim \mathrm{N}_{kpk} \left[\theta_\beta,\Omega_\beta\right] prior means (elements θβ\theta_\beta) set E(Πℓ(,j))={κif ℓ=1andi=j0otherwiseκ={κlevelif variableiisinlevelκΔif variableiisindifference\begin{aligned} \mathrm{E}\left(\Pi_{\ell}^{(,j)}\right)&= \begin{cases} \kappa & \text{} \ell = 1 \ \mathrm{} \ = j \\ 0 & \mathrm{otherwise} \end{cases}\\ \kappa&= \begin{cases} \kappa^{level} & \text{} \mathrm{variable} \ \ \mathrm{level} \\ \kappa^{\Delta} & \text{}\mathrm{variable} \ \ \mathrm{difference} \end{cases}\\ \end{aligned} , autoregressive coefficient Πℓ(,j)\Pi_{\ell}^{(,j)} element (,j)\left(,j\right) Πℓ\Pi_{\ell} ℓ=1,…,p\ell=1,\dots,p. , Minnesota prior sets prior means elements β\beta 00, except elements relate first lags variables, set κ\kappa. variable ii level (e.g. nominal interest rate), κ=κlevel\kappa=\kappa^{level}, typical choices κlevel\kappa^{level} 11 0.90.9. Evaluating equations prior means, equation ii becomes random walk κlevel=1\kappa^{level}=1 persistent stationary AR(1) process κlevel=0.9\kappa^{level}=0.9. Since steady state exists process stationary, 0.90.9 recommended steady-state BVAR. variable ii difference (e.g. output growth), κ=κΔ\kappa=\kappa^{\Delta}, common choice κΔ\kappa^{\Delta} 00, .e. equation ii becomes (evaluating prior means) random walk expressed first differences. differenced variable still shows degree persistence (can examined ACF plot), suitable value κΔ\kappa^{\Delta} can (example) 0.60.6 instead 00. Moving prior variances, Ωβ\Omega_\beta diagonal matrix containing prior variances elements β\beta. specified Var(Πℓ(,j))={(λ1ℓλ3)2if =j(λ1λ2σiℓλ3σj)2if ≠j\mathrm{Var}\left(\Pi_{\ell}^{(,j)}\right)= \begin{cases} \left(\frac{\lambda_1}{\ell^{\lambda_3}}\right)^2 & \text{} = j \\ \left(\frac{\lambda_1 \lambda_2\sigma_i}{\ell^{\lambda_3}\sigma_j}\right)^2& \text{} \neq j \end{cases} λ1\lambda_1, λ2\lambda_2, λ3\lambda_3 scalar hyperparameters known overall tightness, cross-equation tightness lag decay rate. Furthermore, σi2\sigma_i^2 (,)(,):th element Σu\Sigma_u, know therefore replace estimate. package, replaced least squares residual variance univariate autoregression variable ii pp lags (including constant dummy/trend variable applicable). Moving Ψ\Psi, prior use vec(Ψ)∼Nkq[θΨ,ΩΨ]\mathrm{vec}(\Psi) \sim \mathrm{N}_{kq}\left[\theta_\Psi,\Omega_\Psi\right] really core steady-state BVAR model. θΨ\theta_\Psi, specify prior beliefs location steady state, ΩΨ\Omega_\Psi, assume diagonal matrix, specify degree certainty prior beliefs. Finally, prior Σu\Sigma_u usual noninformative Jeffreys prior p(Σu)∝|Σu|−(k+1)/2p(\Sigma_u) \propto\left|\Sigma_u \right|^{-(k+1)/2} However, user wants, inverse Wishart prior can used instead Σu∼IW(V0,m0)\Sigma_u \sim \mathrm{IW}(V_0,m_0) V0V_0 scale matrix m0≥k+2m_0\geq k+2 number degrees freedom. uninformative prior can (package) specified setting V0=(m0−k−1)Σ̂uV_0=(m_0-k-1)\hat{\Sigma}_u Σ̂u\hat{\Sigma}_u least squares estimate VAR(pp) (including constant dummy/trend variable applicable), m0=k+2m_0=k+2. package also allows stochastic volatility (Random Walk AR1 specifications), let covariance matrix innovations vary time time-varying covariance matrix Σu,t\Sigma_{u,t} (details found respective vignettes). estimate model Section 4.1 Villani (2009) perform impulse response analysis forecasting, simply run following code:
code: library(SteadyStateBVAR) data("Villani2009") yt <- Villani2009 yt <- ts(yt[1:102, ], start = start(yt), frequency = frequency(yt))  bvar_obj <- bvar(data = yt)  bp <- which(time(yt) == 1992.75) dum_var <- c(rep(1,bp), rep(0,nrow(yt)-bp))  bvar_obj <- setup(bvar_obj,                   p=4,                   deterministic = "constant_and_dummy",                   dummy = dum_var)  lambda_1 <- 0.2 lambda_2 <- 0.5 lambda_3 <- 1.0  #fol_pm = first own lag prior means fol_pm=c(0,   #delta y_f          0,   #pi_f          0.9, #i_f          0,   #delta y          0,   #pi          0.9, #i          0.9  #q          )  #psi_1 = Psi col 1 #psi_2 = Psi col 2  # default is 95% prior probability ('interval=0.95') theta_Psi <-    c(   ppi( 2.00,  3.00,  annualized_growthrate=TRUE)$mean,   #psi_1: delta y_f   ppi( 1.50,  2.50,  annualized_growthrate=TRUE)$mean,   #psi_1: pi_f   ppi( 4.50,  5.50,  annualized_growthrate=FALSE)$mean,  #psi_1: i_f   ppi( 2.00,  2.50,  annualized_growthrate=TRUE)$mean,   #psi_1: delta y   ppi( 1.70,  2.30,  annualized_growthrate=TRUE)$mean,   #psi_1: pi   ppi( 4.00,  4.50,  annualized_growthrate=FALSE)$mean,  #psi_1: i   ppi( 3.85,  4.00,  annualized_growthrate=FALSE)$mean,  #psi_1: q   ppi(-1.00,  1.00,  annualized_growthrate=TRUE)$mean,   #psi_2: delta y_f   ppi( 1.50,  2.50,  annualized_growthrate=TRUE)$mean,   #psi_2: pi_f   ppi( 1.50,  2.50,  annualized_growthrate=FALSE)$mean,  #psi_2: i_f   ppi(-1.00,  1.00,  annualized_growthrate=TRUE)$mean,   #psi_2: delta y   ppi( 4.30,  5.70,  annualized_growthrate=TRUE)$mean,   #psi_2: pi   ppi( 3.00,  5.50,  annualized_growthrate=FALSE)$mean,  #psi_2: i   ppi(-0.50,  0.50,  annualized_growthrate=FALSE)$mean   #psi_2: q   )  Omega_Psi <-    diag(   c(   ppi( 2.00,  3.00,  annualized_growthrate=TRUE)$var,    #psi_1: delta y_f   ppi( 1.50,  2.50,  annualized_growthrate=TRUE)$var,    #psi_1: pi_f   ppi( 4.50,  5.50,  annualized_growthrate=FALSE)$var,   #psi_1: i_f   ppi( 2.00,  2.50,  annualized_growthrate=TRUE)$var,    #psi_1: delta y   ppi( 1.70,  2.30,  annualized_growthrate=TRUE)$var,    #psi_1: pi   ppi( 4.00,  4.50,  annualized_growthrate=FALSE)$var,   #psi_1: i   ppi( 3.85,  4.00,  annualized_growthrate=FALSE)$var,   #psi_1: q   ppi(-1.00,  1.00,  annualized_growthrate=TRUE)$var,    #psi_2: delta y_f   ppi( 1.50,  2.50,  annualized_growthrate=TRUE)$var,    #psi_2: pi_f   ppi( 1.50,  2.50,  annualized_growthrate=FALSE)$var,   #psi_2: i_f   ppi(-1.00,  1.00,  annualized_growthrate=TRUE)$var,    #psi_2: delta y   ppi( 4.30,  5.70,  annualized_growthrate=TRUE)$var,    #psi_2: pi   ppi( 3.00,  5.50,  annualized_growthrate=FALSE)$var,   #psi_2: i   ppi(-0.50,  0.50,  annualized_growthrate=FALSE)$var    #psi_2: q   )   )  bvar_obj <- priors(bvar_obj,                    lambda_1,                    lambda_2,                    lambda_3,                    fol_pm,                    theta_Psi,                    Omega_Psi,                    Jeffrey=TRUE)  p <- bvar_obj$setup$p k <- bvar_obj$setup$k kf <- 3 #foreign variables  restriction_matrix <- matrix(1, k*p, k)  for(i in 1:p){   rows <- ((i-1)*k + kf + 1) : (i*k)   cols <- 1:kf   restriction_matrix[rows, cols] <- 0 } #block exogeneity for foreign variables bvar_obj <- restrict_beta(bvar_obj, restriction_matrix)  bvar_obj$predict$H <- 12 bvar_obj$predict$d_pred <- cbind(rep(1, 12), 0)  bvar_obj <- fit(bvar_obj,                 iter = 20000,                 warmup = 10000,                 chains = 4)  summary(bvar_obj)  fcst <- forecast(bvar_obj,                  ci = 0.95,                  fcst_type = "mean",                  growth_rate_idx = c(4,5), #annual inflation/real GDP growth instead of QoQ                  plot_idx = c(4,5,6))  irf <- IRF(bvar_obj,            H=20,            response=5,#inflation            shock=6, #interest rate            type="median",            method="OIRF",            ci=0.68,            growth_rate_idx=5) #annual inflation instead of QoQ

title: Estimation of the steady-state Bayesian Vector Autoregression model using Hamiltonian Monte Carlo
text: Clark, T. E. (2011). Real-Time Density Forecasts Bayesian Vector Autoregressions Stochastic Volatility. Journal Business & Economic Statistics. 29(3), pp. 327–341. Dieppe, ., Legrand, R., van Roye, B. (2018). Bayesian Estimation, Analysis Regression (BEAR) Toolbox Technical guide. European Central Bank. Karlsson, S. (2013). Forecasting Bayesian Vector Autoregression. : Elliott, G. Timmerman, . (eds) Handbook Economic Forecasting. Elsevier B.V. Vol 2, Part B., pp. 791-897. Villani, M. (2009). Steady-state priors vector autoregressions. Journal Applied Econometrics. 24(4), pp. 630-650.

what: Impulse Response Functions for a BVAR model — IRF
title: Impulse Response Functions for a BVAR model — IRF
text: Computes plots impulse response functions (IRFs) fitted bvar object. Supports orthogonalised (OIRF) generalised (GIRF) impulse responses, optional conversion annual growth rates.

title: Impulse Response Functions for a BVAR model — IRF
code: IRF(   x,   H = 16,   response = NULL,   shock = NULL,   type = c("median", "mean"),   method = c("OIRF", "GIRF"),   ci = 0.95,   t = NULL,   growth_rate_idx = NULL )

title: Impulse Response Functions for a BVAR model — IRF
text: x bvar object passed fit. H Integer. forecast horizon IRF. Default 16. response Integer. Index response variable plot. NULL (default), responses plotted. shock Integer. Index shock variable plot. NULL (default), shocks plotted. type Character. Whether use "median" "mean" point estimate. Default "median". method Character. IRF method: "OIRF" orthogonalised "GIRF" generalised impulse responses. Default "OIRF". ci Numeric. credible interval width. Default 0.95. t Integer. Time index covariance matrix using stochastic volatility models. NULL (default), last time period used. growth_rate_idx Integer vector. Indices variables convert annual growth rates. NULL (default), conversion applied.

title: Impulse Response Functions for a BVAR model — IRF
text: list three arrays: point estimate IRF, lower, upper credible bounds, dimension k x k x (H+1).

title: Impulse Response Functions for a BVAR model — IRF
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ model <- bvar(data = my_data) model <- setup(model, p = 2, deterministic = "constant") model <- priors(model) model <- fit(model) irf <- IRF(model, H = 16, method = "OIRF") } # }

what: Create a BVAR model object — bvar
title: Create a BVAR model object — bvar
text: Initialises Bayesian Vector Autoregression (BVAR) model object. starting point models SteadyStateBVAR. creation, pass object sequentially setup, priors, fit build estimate model.

title: Create a BVAR model object — bvar
code: bvar(data)

title: Create a BVAR model object — bvar
text: data numeric matrix time series data column variable row time period.

title: Create a BVAR model object — bvar
text: object class bvar.

title: Create a BVAR model object — bvar
code: yt <- matrix(rnorm(40, 0, 1), 20, 2)  bvar_obj <- bvar(data = yt)

what: Conditional forecasts from a fitted BVAR model — conditional_forecast
title: Conditional forecasts from a fitted BVAR model — conditional_forecast
text: Computes plots conditional forecasts fitted bvar object. Conditions imposed specific variables specific horizons using method Waggoner Zha (1999). conditional unconditional forecasts plotted comparison.

title: Conditional forecasts from a fitted BVAR model — conditional_forecast
code: conditional_forecast(   bvar_obj,   conditions,   ci = 0.95,   fcst_type = c("mean", "median"),   growth_rate_idx = NULL,   plot_idx = NULL )

title: Conditional forecasts from a fitted BVAR model — conditional_forecast
text: bvar_obj bvar object passed fit. conditions data frame three columns: var (integer index conditioned variable), horizon (integer forecast horizon condition imposed), value (numeric value variable conditioned ). ci Numeric. credible interval width. Default 0.95. fcst_type Character. Whether use "mean" "median" point forecast. Default "mean". growth_rate_idx Integer vector. Indices variables convert annual growth rates. NULL (default), variables plotted levels. plot_idx Integer vector. Indices variables plot. NULL (default), variables plotted.

title: Conditional forecasts from a fitted BVAR model — conditional_forecast
text: list three matrices: forecast, lower, upper, dimension H x k, well cond_draws, array posterior conditional forecast draws.

title: Conditional forecasts from a fitted BVAR model — conditional_forecast
text: Waggoner, D. F. Zha, T. (1999). Conditional forecasts dynamic multivariate models. Review Economics Statistics, 81(4), 639-651.

title: Conditional forecasts from a fitted BVAR model — conditional_forecast
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Condition on variable 1 being 2.0 at horizon 4 conditions <- data.frame(var = 1, horizon = 4, value = 2.0) model <- bvar(data = my_data) model <- setup(model, p = 2, deterministic = "constant") model <- priors(model) model <- fit(model) conditional_forecast(model, conditions = conditions) } # }

what: Estimate a Bayesian VAR model using Stan — fit
title: Estimate a Bayesian VAR model using Stan — fit
text: Runs Stan estimate BVAR model using data, setup, priors stored bvar object. Supports standard homoscedastic steady-state BVAR well stochastic volatility specifications (RW AR(1)).

title: Estimate a Bayesian VAR model using Stan — fit
code: fit(   x,   iter = 5000,   warmup = 2500,   chains = 2,   cores = min(chains, parallel::detectCores()),   auto_write = TRUE )

title: Estimate a Bayesian VAR model using Stan — fit
text: x bvar object passed setup priors. iter Integer. Total number MCMC iterations per chain. Default 5000. warmup Integer. Number warmup (burn-) iterations per chain. Default 2500. chains Integer. Number MCMC chains. Default 2. cores Integer. Number CPU cores used sampling. auto_write Logical. Whether enable rstan auto-write behavior.

title: Estimate a Bayesian VAR model using Stan — fit
text: bvar object containing: fit$stan Stan fit object posterior_means Posterior means key parameters

title: Estimate a Bayesian VAR model using Stan — fit
text: Forecast inputs must provided x$predict$H x$predict$d_pred calling fit().

title: Estimate a Bayesian VAR model using Stan — fit
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ yt <- matrix(rnorm(40, 0, 1), 20, 2)  bvar_obj <- bvar(data = yt)  bvar_obj <- setup(bvar_obj, p=1)  bvar_obj <- priors(bvar_obj,                    theta_Psi = rep(0, 2),                    Omega_Psi = diag(0.1, 2, 2))  bvar_obj$predict$H <- 1 bvar_obj$predict$d_pred <- matrix(1)  bvar_obj <- fit(bvar_obj,                 iter = 200,                 warmup = 50,                 chains = 1,                 cores = 1,                 auto_write = FALSE) } # }

what: Forecast from a fitted BVAR model — forecast
title: Forecast from a fitted BVAR model — forecast
text: Computes plots forecasts fitted bvar object. Posterior predictive draws Stan used construct point forecasts credible intervals. Optionally converts level forecasts annual growth rates selected variables.

title: Forecast from a fitted BVAR model — forecast
code: forecast(   x,   ci = 0.95,   fcst_type = c("mean", "median"),   growth_rate_idx = NULL,   plot_idx = NULL,   show_all = FALSE )

title: Forecast from a fitted BVAR model — forecast
text: x bvar object passed fit. ci Numeric. credible interval width. Default 0.95. fcst_type Character. Whether use "mean" "median" point forecast. Default "mean". growth_rate_idx Integer vector. Indices variables convert annual growth rates. NULL (default), variables plotted levels. plot_idx Integer vector. Indices variables plot. NULL (default), variables plotted. show_all Logical. FALSE (default), last two years history shown alongside forecast. TRUE, full history shown.

title: Forecast from a fitted BVAR model — forecast
text: list three matrices: forecast, lower, upper, dimension H x k H forecast horizon k number variables.

title: Forecast from a fitted BVAR model — forecast
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ model <- bvar(data = my_data) model <- setup(model, p = 2, deterministic = "constant") model <- priors(model) model <- fit(model) fcst <- forecast(model, ci = 0.95, fcst_type = "mean") } # }

what: Prior Probability Interval for a Normal Distribution — ppi
title: Prior Probability Interval for a Normal Distribution — ppi
text: Converts symmetric prior probability interval corresponding mean variance normal distribution. Given lower upper bound define prior probability interval, function recovers implied normal prior parameters. Useful specifying informative priors steady-state (Psi) parameters intuitive way.

title: Prior Probability Interval for a Normal Distribution — ppi
code: ppi(l, u, interval = 0.95, annualized_growthrate = FALSE, freq = 4)

title: Prior Probability Interval for a Normal Distribution — ppi
text: l Numeric. lower bound prior probability interval. u Numeric. upper bound prior probability interval. interval Numeric. prior probability mass within interval. Default 0.95. annualized_growthrate Logical. TRUE, converts interval annualized per-period units dividing freq. Default FALSE. freq Integer. data frequency (e.g. 4 quarterly). used annualized_growthrate = TRUE. Default 4.

title: Prior Probability Interval for a Normal Distribution — ppi
text: list two elements: mean var, giving mean variance implied normal distribution.

title: Prior Probability Interval for a Normal Distribution — ppi
code: # steady-state annualized inflation is between 1% and 3% with 95% probability ppi(l = 1, u = 3, interval = 0.95) #> $mean #> [1] 2 #>  #> $var #> [1] 0.2603178 #>

what: Specify priors for a Bayesian VAR model with optional stochastic volatility — priors
title: Specify priors for a Bayesian VAR model with optional stochastic volatility — priors
text: Defines Minnesota-style priors VAR coefficients priors steady-state parameters. Optionally enables stochastic volatility specification (random walk AR(1)) augments prior object additional SV-related inputs used Stan model. Jeffrey's prior can used error covariance matrix , alternatively, inverse-Wishart prior.

title: Specify priors for a Bayesian VAR model with optional stochastic volatility — priors
code: priors(   x,   lambda_1 = 0.2,   lambda_2 = 0.5,   lambda_3 = 1,   first_own_lag_prior_mean = NULL,   theta_Psi = NULL,   Omega_Psi = NULL,   Jeffrey = TRUE,   SV = FALSE,   SV_type = NULL,   SV_priors = NULL )

title: Specify priors for a Bayesian VAR model with optional stochastic volatility — priors
text: x bvar object passed setup. lambda_1 Numeric. Overall tightness Minnesota prior controlling overall shrinkage. Default 0.2. lambda_2 Numeric. Cross-variable shrinkage parameter controlling shrinkage variables' lags relative lags. Default 0.5. lambda_3 Numeric. Lag decay parameter controlling prior variance decreases lag length. Default 1. first_own_lag_prior_mean Numeric vector length k. Prior means first lag variable. NULL, defaults zero vector. theta_Psi Numeric vector. Prior mean steady-state parameters. NULL, defaults zero vector length k*q. Omega_Psi Numeric matrix. Prior covariance steady-state parameters. NULL, defaults diagonal matrix variance 100. Jeffrey Logical. TRUE, uses Jeffrey's prior error covariance matrix. FALSE, uses inverse-Wishart prior. SV Logical. TRUE, enables stochastic volatility specification. Default FALSE. SV_type Character. Type stochastic volatility model. Must either "RW" "AR". Required SV = TRUE. SV_priors List. User-supplied stochastic volatility prior objects passed directly Stan. Required SV = TRUE.

title: Specify priors for a Bayesian VAR model with optional stochastic volatility — priors
text: bvar object appended priors list containing: theta_beta Prior mean VAR coefficients Omega_beta Prior covariance VAR coefficients theta_Psi Prior mean steady-state parameters Omega_Psi Prior covariance steady-state parameters Jeffrey Indicator Jeffrey's prior usage Sigma_AR Residual variance estimates univariate AR fits m_0 Inverse-Wishart degrees freedom (Jeffrey = FALSE) V_0 Inverse-Wishart scale matrix (Jeffrey = FALSE) SV Logical indicator stochastic volatility model SV_type Stochastic volatility specification type SV_priors User-supplied SV prior list (SV = TRUE)

title: Specify priors for a Bayesian VAR model with optional stochastic volatility — priors
text: function performs following checks: Ensures x valid bvar object Ensures setup exists contains required elements Ensures lambda parameters strictly positive Ensures first_own_lag_prior_mean length k (provided) Ensures theta_Psi Omega_Psi dimensions match (provided) Ensures valid SV_type SV = TRUE Ensures SV_priors provided SV = TRUE

title: Specify priors for a Bayesian VAR model with optional stochastic volatility — priors
code: yt <- matrix(rnorm(40, 0, 1), 20, 2)  bvar_obj <- bvar(data = yt)  bvar_obj <- setup(bvar_obj, p=1)  bvar_obj <- priors(bvar_obj,                    theta_Psi = rep(0, 2),                    Omega_Psi = diag(0.1, 2, 2))

what: Restrict VAR coefficients to zero — restrict_beta
title: Restrict VAR coefficients to zero — restrict_beta
text: Applies zero restrictions VAR coefficient matrix setting corresponding prior variances Omega_beta value near zero. enforces restrictions prior rather hard-coding likelihood, compatible Stan estimation.

title: Restrict VAR coefficients to zero — restrict_beta
code: restrict_beta(x, restriction_matrix)

title: Restrict VAR coefficients to zero — restrict_beta
text: x bvar object passed priors. restriction_matrix numeric matrix dimension k*p x k entries 0 indicate coefficients restricted zero entries 1 indicate unrestricted coefficients.

title: Restrict VAR coefficients to zero — restrict_beta
text: bvar object restriction matrix stored setup Omega_beta updated accordingly.

title: Restrict VAR coefficients to zero — restrict_beta
code: yt <- matrix(rnorm(40, 0, 1), 20, 2)  bvar_obj <- bvar(data = yt)  bvar_obj <- setup(bvar_obj, p=1)  bvar_obj <- priors(bvar_obj,                    theta_Psi = rep(0, 2),                    Omega_Psi = diag(0.1, 2, 2))                     p <- bvar_obj$setup$p k <- bvar_obj$setup$k restriction_matrix <- matrix(1, k*p, k) restriction_matrix[1, 1] <- 0  bvar_obj <- restrict_beta(bvar_obj, restriction_matrix)

what: Set up a BVAR model — setup
title: Set up a BVAR model — setup
text: generic function setting BVAR model. Computes OLS estimates prepares matrices needed prior specification estimation.

title: Set up a BVAR model — setup
code: setup(x, ...)  # S3 method for class 'bvar' setup(   x,   p,   deterministic = c("constant", "constant_and_dummy", "constant_and_trend"),   dummy = NULL,   ... )

title: Set up a BVAR model — setup
text: x bvar object created bvar. ... arguments passed methods. p Integer. lag order VAR. deterministic Character. deterministic component include. One "constant" (default), "constant_and_dummy", "constant_and_trend". dummy Optional numeric vector matrix dummy variables. used deterministic = "constant_and_dummy". Default NULL.

title: Set up a BVAR model — setup
text: bvar object setup list appended.

title: Set up a BVAR model — setup
code: yt <- matrix(rnorm(40, 0, 1), 20, 2)  bvar_obj <- bvar(data = yt)  bvar_obj <- setup(bvar_obj, p = 1)

what: Plot stochastic volatility estimates and forecasts — stochastic_volatility_forecast
title: Plot stochastic volatility estimates and forecasts — stochastic_volatility_forecast
text: Plots posterior distribution stochastic volatility estimation sample forecast horizon fitted bvar object stochastic volatility specification. Supports plotting either log-volatility (log_lambda) posterior standard deviations (sd).

title: Plot stochastic volatility estimates and forecasts — stochastic_volatility_forecast
code: stochastic_volatility_forecast(   x,   ci = 0.95,   vol = "log_lambda",   plot_idx = NULL,   xlim = NULL,   ylim = NULL )

title: Plot stochastic volatility estimates and forecasts — stochastic_volatility_forecast
text: x bvar object passed fit stochastic volatility specification. ci Numeric. credible interval width. Default 0.95. vol Character. volatility measure plot. Either "log_lambda" (default) log-volatility "sd" posterior standard deviations. plot_idx Integer vector. Indices variables plot. NULL (default), variables plotted. xlim Numeric vector length 2. x-axis limits. NULL (default), limits set automatically. ylim Numeric vector length 2. y-axis limits. NULL (default), limits set automatically.

title: Plot stochastic volatility estimates and forecasts — stochastic_volatility_forecast
text: list two components: estimated List containing posterior summaries estimation sample: mean: Matrix posterior means (T × k) lower: Matrix lower credible bounds (T × k) upper: Matrix upper credible bounds (T × k) predicted List containing posterior summaries forecast horizon: mean: Matrix predictive means ((T+H) × k) lower: Matrix predictive lower credible bounds upper: Matrix predictive upper credible bounds

title: Plot stochastic volatility estimates and forecasts — stochastic_volatility_forecast
text: function also returns posterior summaries -sample estimates --sample forecasts.

title: Plot stochastic volatility estimates and forecasts — stochastic_volatility_forecast
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ model <- bvar(data = my_data) model <- setup(model, p = 2, deterministic = "constant") model <- priors(model) model$SV <- TRUE model$SV_type <- "RW" model <- fit(model)  res <- stochastic_volatility_forecast(model, ci = 0.95) str(res) } # }

what: Summarise a fitted BVAR model — summary.bvar
title: Summarise a fitted BVAR model — summary.bvar
text: Computes posterior means model parameters fitted bvar object. parameters returned depend model specification: standard homoscedastic models return beta, Psi, Sigma; stochastic volatility models additionally return volatility parameters.

title: Summarise a fitted BVAR model — summary.bvar
code: # S3 method for class 'bvar' summary(object, pars = NULL, ...)  # S3 method for class 'summary.bvar' print(x, ...)

title: Summarise a fitted BVAR model — summary.bvar
text: object bvar object passed fit. pars Character vector parameter names include. NULL (default), parameters returned. ... arguments passed methods. x summary.bvar object returned summary.bvar.

title: Summarise a fitted BVAR model — summary.bvar
text: object class summary.bvar.

title: Summarise a fitted BVAR model — summary.bvar
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ yt <- matrix(rnorm(40, 0, 1), 20, 2)  bvar_obj <- bvar(data = yt)  bvar_obj <- setup(bvar_obj, p=1)  bvar_obj <- priors(bvar_obj,                    theta_Psi = rep(0, 2),                    Omega_Psi = diag(0.1, 2, 2))  bvar_obj$predict$H <- 1 bvar_obj$predict$d_pred <- matrix(1)  bvar_obj <- fit(bvar_obj,                 iter = 200,                 warmup = 50,                 chains = 1,                 cores = 1,                 auto_write = FALSE)                  summary(bvar_obj) } # }
